title: Range-based composition basics

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Range-based composition basics; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C++ ranges combine range-aware algorithms and composable lazy views, with concepts checking operations and borrowed or owning relationships determining lifetime safety.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A filter view over a live vector is iterated while the vector remains alive and structurally unchanged. Your goal is to build and explain a simple filtered and transformed range pipeline. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iterators and invalidation; Overloading and default arguments; Lambdas and captures. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C++ ranges combine range-aware algorithms and composable lazy views, with concepts checking operations and borrowed or owning relationships determining lifetime safety. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Views often do not own elements, temporary or mutated sources can dangle or invalidate, lazy evaluation defers work, and not every range is common or sized. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Every range view owns a copied collection, so it remains valid after the original container is destroyed.

includes: Read and use simple C++20 range views without hiding ownership or lifetime problems.
Build and explain a simple filtered and transformed range pipeline.
excludes: Views often do not own elements, temporary or mutated sources can dangle or invalidate, lazy evaluation defers work, and not every range is common or sized.

outcomes: Build and explain a simple filtered and transformed range pipeline.

examples.title: Range-based composition basics: defined case
examples.situation: A filter view over a live vector is iterated while the vector remains alive and structurally unchanged.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A filter view over a live vector is iterated while the vector remains alive and structurally unchanged.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Views commonly borrow and evaluate lazily; prove the source and captured state outlive iteration or return an owning result.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Views commonly borrow and evaluate lazily; prove the source and captured state outlive iteration or return an owning result.

examples.title: Range-based composition basics: boundary case
examples.situation: A view pipeline is not returned when it would refer to a local source destroyed at function exit.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A view pipeline is not returned when it would refer to a local source destroyed at function exit.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Views often do not own elements, temporary or mutated sources can dangle or invalidate, lazy evaluation defers work, and not every range is common or sized.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Every range view owns a copied collection, so it remains valid after the original container is destroyed.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Build and explain a simple filtered and transformed range pipeline.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every range view owns a copied collection, so it remains valid after the original container is destroyed.
misconceptions.correction: Views commonly borrow and evaluate lazily; prove the source and captured state outlive iteration or return an owning result.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: Every range view owns a copied collection, so it remains valid after the original container is destroyed.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Range-based composition basics: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
